_meta.notes: v0.7: Added character fields to quiz cards. Added sentence_build and image_match quiz types. Added image fields to teach cards.

cards.body: The most common greeting in Tagalog is kumusta.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Kumusta' comes from Spanish '¿Cómo está?' It's been Filipino for centuries now. Pronounced koo-MUS-tah.

cards.body: Add po and it becomes respectful. You already know this pattern.

example.note: Use kumusta po with elders, strangers, and anyone you'd show respect to.

cards.body: The most common response is mabuti, which means 'good' or 'fine.'

example.note: 'Naman' softens the sentence. It's like adding 'actually' or 'though.' Very natural in Filipino.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Naman' has no direct English translation. It softens, emphasizes, or adds a sense of 'for my part.' Filipinos use it constantly.

cards.prompt: What does 'kumusta' mean?
cards.explanation: Kumusta means 'How are you?' From Spanish 'Cómo está.'

cards.body: You can also keep it casual. 'Okay lang' (just okay) or 'Okay naman' (I'm alright) are perfectly natural responses.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Ikaw' means 'you.' In casual speech, it often gets shortened to just 'ka.'

cards.prompt: Your lola asks 'Kumusta?' How do you respond?
cards.explanation: Lola is an elder. 'Mabuti naman po' shows respect.

cards.prompt: Your pare asks 'Kumusta?' What's the most natural response?
cards.explanation: Close friend, same age. No po needed. 'Okay lang, ikaw?' is casual and natural.

cards.body: Filipinos also greet by time of day. They all start with the same word: magandang.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Maganda' literally means 'beautiful.' When you say 'magandang umaga,' you're really saying 'beautiful morning.' Filipino greetings are poetic.

cards.prompt: How do you say 'good morning'?
cards.explanation: Umaga = morning. Magandang umaga = good morning.

cards.prompt: Which one is 'umaga'?
cards.explanation: Umaga = morning. The sunrise scene.

cards.body: Add po to make any time-of-day greeting respectful.

example.note: Same rule as everything else. Po makes it respectful.

cards.prompt: Good afternoon (respectful): 'Magandang ___ po.'
cards.explanation: Hapon = afternoon. Magandang hapon po.

cards.prompt: It's 8 PM. You arrive at your tita's house. What do you say?
cards.explanation: 8 PM is evening. Gabi = evening/night. And po because she's your tita.

cards.body: There's a greeting in Filipino that confuses non-Filipinos. It sounds like a question about food, but it's really just 'how are you?'
cards.highlight: Kumain ka na ba? = Have you eaten?

cards.body: Filipinos ask 'Kumain ka na ba?' the same way Americans say 'How's it going?' They don't actually need to know what you ate.

example.context: Your tita sees you at a family gathering
example.translation: Have you eaten? (functioning as: How are you? / Are you taken care of?)

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: This greeting comes from Filipino hospitality culture. Making sure someone has eaten is the most basic way to show you care. It's love expressed as a question.

cards.body: With po, it becomes even more caring.
If you have eaten, you can say 'Kumain na po' (I already ate, respectful). If not, 'Hindi pa po' (not yet, respectful).

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Na' means 'already' or 'now.' 'Kumain na' = already ate. 'Kain na!' = eat now! Small word, big job.

cards.prompt: When a Filipino asks 'Kumain ka na ba?', what are they really doing?
options: Greeting you / checking in on you
cards.explanation: It functions as a greeting. It's the Filipino way of asking if you're okay and taken care of.

cards.prompt: Your lola asks 'Kumain ka na ba?' and you already ate. What do you say?
cards.explanation: Lola is an elder. 'Kumain na po' is respectful and answers her question.

cards.prompt: Your lola asks 'Kumain ka na ba?' and you haven't eaten yet. What do you say?
cards.explanation: 'Hindi pa po' means 'not yet' (respectful). 'Pa' adds 'yet.' 'Hindi po' without 'pa' just means 'no.'

cards.body: In real life, Filipinos often combine greetings. A time-of-day greeting first, then kumusta.

example.context: Arriving at your lola's house in the morning

cards.body: With friends, it's much simpler. 'Kumusta?' or even just 'Musta?' is enough.

example.context: Seeing your pare at a coffee shop

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: 'Musta' is the casual/slang version of 'kumusta.' Like 'whatup' is to 'what's up.' Never use musta with elders.

cards.body: At family gatherings, remember the order: mano po first (if elders are present), then greet, then chat.

example.context: Arriving at a family party in the evening
example.sentence: Mano po, Lolo. Magandang gabi po!

cards.prompt: It's morning. You see your teacher. What's a good greeting?
cards.explanation: Teacher = authority. Morning = umaga. 'Magandang umaga po!' is correct and respectful.

cards.prompt: You bump into your mare at the mall. How do you greet her?
cards.explanation: Close female friend. Casual is fine. 'Musta, mare?' is natural.

cards.prompt: Greet Lolo
cards.source_text: You just arrived at Lolo's house in the evening. Greet him properly.
cards.explanation: 'Magandang gabi po! Kumusta po?' Combining a time-of-day greeting with kumusta.

cards.prompt: Build a respectful morning greeting.
cards.explanation: 'Magandang umaga po!' Po goes at the end of the greeting.

cards.prompt: You arrive at your lola's house in the evening. What do you do first?
cards.explanation: Mano po first with elders, then greet. The gesture comes before the words.

cards.prompt: Casual 'how are you' to a friend: '___?'
cards.explanation: Musta is casual kumusta. Only with friends, never with elders.

cards.prompt: What does 'kumusta' mean?
cards.explanation: Kumusta = How are you? From Spanish 'Cómo está.'

cards.prompt: Good evening (respectful): 'Magandang ___ po.'
cards.explanation: Gabi = evening/night. Magandang gabi po.

cards.prompt: Your tita asks 'Kumusta?' How do you respond?
cards.explanation: Tita is an elder. 'Mabuti naman po' is the respectful response.

cards.prompt: A coworker you're close with asks 'Kumain ka na ba?' and you haven't eaten. What do you say?
cards.explanation: Close coworker, same age. No po needed. 'Hindi pa' means 'not yet.'

cards.prompt: Greet your teacher
cards.source_text: It's 2 PM. You see your teacher in the hallway. Greet them properly.
cards.explanation: 'Magandang hapon po!' Hapon = afternoon.

cards.prompt: When a Filipino asks 'Kumain ka na ba?', they're really...
cards.explanation: It functions as a greeting. Asking about food is how Filipinos show care.

cards.prompt: Which greeting would you NEVER use with your lola?
cards.explanation: 'Musta' is casual slang. Never with elders. Kumusta po is the respectful version.

cards.prompt: Respectful 'how are you': '___ po?'
cards.explanation: Kumusta po? The respectful way to ask how someone is.

cards.prompt: It's 3 PM. You're greeting your boss. What do you say?
cards.explanation: 3 PM = afternoon. Boss = authority. 'Magandang hapon po!'

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete session. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice. E.g., 'User struggled with time-of-day greetings (confused hapon and gabi). Focus practice on greeting selection by context.'

alam_mo_ba: 'Kumusta' comes from Spanish '¿Cómo está?' It arrived with colonisers but became completely Filipino over 400 years.
There's no standard 'goodbye' greeting in Tagalog. Filipinos usually just say 'sige' (okay/go ahead) or 'ingat' (take care) when leaving.
'Maganda' means beautiful. So 'magandang umaga' literally translates to 'beautiful morning.' Filipino greetings are more poetic than English ones.
The 'Kumain ka na ba?' greeting reflects a culture where food is love. Feeding someone is the highest form of Filipino hospitality.
In some regions, 'Namamasyal ka?' (Are you out for a walk?) is used as a greeting instead of kumusta. Greetings vary across the Philippines.
completion_reward.alam_mo_ba_display: Show 1 random fun fact on lesson completion screen. On replay, show a different one.

progress_ring.display: Ring animates from X/5 to (X+1)/5 on session completion
